title: Mean annual precipitation and topsoil organic carbon across the Cerrado
abstract_text: In a study of 520 Oxisols across the Cerrado biome, mean annual precipitation explained 48 percent of the variance in topsoil organic carbon. Rainfall drives litter input via net primary productivity, which is the dominant control on topsoil SOC in this biome. Mean annual temperature was negatively associated with SOC after controlling for precipitation, consistent with faster microbial decomposition in warmer sites.

title: Clay content stabilises organic carbon in ferralic soils
abstract_text: Across 380 profiles from the Brazilian savanna, clay content emerged as the strongest predictor of topsoil SOC after adjusting for climate.  Organo-mineral associations with Fe and Al oxides in kaolinitic clays protect SOC from microbial mineralisation. Sandy soils, by contrast, lost organic carbon rapidly once native vegetation was removed.

title: Fire frequency and soil organic carbon dynamics in Cerrado sensu stricto
abstract_text: Long-term fire suppression experiments in Cerrado sensu stricto increased topsoil SOC by 15 percent over 18 years, while the opposite trend was observed under biennial burning. Fire volatilises surface organic matter and reduces litter inputs, although it may increase pyrogenic carbon fractions.

title: Land-use change from native Cerrado to soybean cropland
abstract_text: A chronosequence study of 45 paired sites showed that conversion from native Cerrado to soybean monoculture reduced topsoil SOC by 28 percent within ten years. Bulk density increased with continuous mechanised tillage, and clay-associated SOC declined faster than particulate SOC fractions.

title: Topographic controls on SOC redistribution in a Cerrado catchment
abstract_text: Within a 12 km² catchment, slope gradient was negatively related to topsoil SOC, while topographic wetness index (TWI) was positively related. Steeper slopes experience higher erosion, transporting organic-rich topsoil downslope; low-lying TWI-rich positions accumulate both water and organic matter.

title: pH, CEC and soil organic carbon in weathered Cerrado Latosols
abstract_text: Weathered Cerrado Latosols are typically acidic (pH 4.5-5.5) with low CEC.  In liming experiments, raising pH from 4.8 to 5.8 increased CEC by 35 percent and, over six years, increased SOC by 12 percent, mediated by enhanced root biomass and litter input.

title: Depth-decay of organic carbon under contrasting native vegetation
abstract_text: Topsoil (0-10 cm) SOC was 2.3-fold higher under cerradão (forest physiognomy) than under campo limpo (grassland).  Below 40 cm, the contrast disappeared, suggesting deep SOC is dominated by root-derived carbon that is less sensitive to canopy cover.

title: Parent-material lithology and clay mineralogy
abstract_text: Soils on basalt-derived parent materials have significantly higher clay content than those on sandstone; this lithological control propagates to SOC via the clay-stabilisation pathway (Ferreira & Silva 2017).

title: Drought events and topsoil carbon losses
abstract_text: Drought years (2015, 2016) in central Brazil reduced topsoil SOC by 7 percent in monitored sites.  Drought slowed microbial decomposition transiently but the dominant pathway was reduced productivity and therefore reduced litter input.

title: Elevation-mediated temperature gradients and SOC
abstract_text: Along an elevation transect from 400 to 1100 m.a.s.l. in the central Brazilian Plateau, each 100 m of rise was associated with 0.6 °C cooler mean annual temperature and 7 percent higher topsoil SOC, consistent with reduced decomposition at altitude.

title: Nitrogen fertilisation effects on SOC in tropical pastures
abstract_text: A 10-year N-fertilisation trial in Brachiaria pasture raised above-ground biomass by 40 percent and topsoil SOC by 18 percent. Root biomass also increased proportionally.

title: Grazing intensity reduces topsoil organic carbon in savanna pastures
abstract_text: Heavy grazing (>2 AU/ha) reduced topsoil SOC by 15 percent relative to lightly grazed plots over 12 years. Compaction (bulk density +9 percent) and reduced litter return are the main mechanisms.

title: No-till cropping systems and SOC recovery
abstract_text: No-till soybean-maize rotations recovered 40 percent of the SOC lost to conventional tillage within eight years, primarily through reduced bulk density and restored aggregate stability.

title: Legume cover crops increase topsoil nitrogen and SOC
abstract_text: Incorporation of Crotalaria as winter cover crop increased topsoil N by 22 percent and SOC by 9 percent over four years, with clay soils showing a larger response than sandy soils.

title: Silvopasture integration and SOC stocks
abstract_text: Integrating Eucalyptus rows into Brachiaria pasture increased 0-30 cm SOC stocks by 14 Mg C/ha over nine years, relative to grass-only controls.

title: Bulk density as a predictor of SOC storage
abstract_text: In a meta-analysis of 640 Cerrado profiles, bulk density correlated negatively with topsoil SOC (r = -0.61), partly because tillage both compacts and mineralises SOC.

title: Water erosion in riverine agricultural landscapes
abstract_text: RUSLE-based estimates indicate that soybean fields on slopes >8 percent lose 12 Mg of topsoil per hectare per year, depleting topsoil SOC at rates 3-4 times higher than nearby native vegetation.

title: Precipitation seasonality and weathering rates
abstract_text: Highly seasonal Cerrado climates (dry season 5-6 months) promote deep kaolinitic weathering, producing clay-rich B horizons below 1 m but nutrient-poor topsoils. Weathering intensity correlates with elevation through rainfall gradients.

title: NDVI as a proxy for above-ground biomass across Cerrado
abstract_text: Dry-season NDVI explained 73 percent of the variance in above- ground biomass across 210 Cerrado plots. NDVI was in turn positively related to mean annual precipitation and negatively related to dry-season temperature.

title: Aspect and solar radiation effects on SOC
abstract_text: North-facing slopes received 12 percent more solar radiation than south-facing slopes across a Brasília-area catchment, translating into higher evapotranspiration and 8 percent lower topsoil SOC on north-facing sites.

title: Soil texture as mediator of nitrogen cycling
abstract_text: A 250-profile dataset showed that clay and silt content jointly explained 58% of variation in total soil nitrogen in Cerrado topsoils, with sand acting as a negative predictor.

title: Forest-to-pasture conversion halves topsoil N
abstract_text: A chronosequence of pastures converted from Cerradão forest showed topsoil N halved within 15 years, with concomitant SOC losses of 32%.

title: Termite activity and soil aggregation
abstract_text: Termite galleries were associated with 20% higher macro-aggregate stability and 9% higher topsoil SOC in undisturbed Cerrado, through bioturbation-driven organic matter burial.

title: Biochar amendments in Cerrado soils
abstract_text: Single applications of 20 Mg/ha biochar increased topsoil SOC by 35% within three years, with no significant change in pH or CEC.

title: Post-fire recovery of soil physical properties
abstract_text: Five years after an unplanned fire, bulk density returned to pre-fire values and SOC recovered 85% of pre-fire levels through vegetation regrowth.

title: Soil moisture and CEC interactions
abstract_text: Dry-season soil moisture positively correlates with CEC in clay-rich Latosols (r=0.48), through moisture-mediated organo-mineral complexation.

title: Topographic wetness index and soil formation
abstract_text: High-TWI valleys accumulated an additional 8 cm of A-horizon over 10 000 years relative to ridgetops, via lateral translocation of fine particles and organic matter.

title: Atmospheric N deposition in urban Cerrado
abstract_text: Within 50 km of Brasília, atmospheric N deposition (8-12 kg N/ha/yr) increased topsoil N by 15% over 10 years without altering SOC stocks.

title: Termite mound-derived clay enrichment
abstract_text: Termite mounds concentrate clay by a factor of 1.6 relative to surrounding soil, creating local microsites with higher SOC and CEC.

title: Seasonal flooding in Vereda wetlands
abstract_text: Seasonally flooded Veredas of the Cerrado accumulate organic-rich Histic horizons; topsoil SOC reaches 8-12% (by mass), 3-4x the upland Cerrado average.
